game_name: Musya: The Classic Japanese Tale of Horror

design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate caverns as pikeman Imoto, defeat boss enemies to acquire spell scrolls, descend deeper into abyss to rescue maiden Shizuka
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Health management (16 units per life) balanced against resource scarcity; progression gated by boss defeats that unlock new spell abilities
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Japanese folk hero descending into supernatural underworld, acquiring mystical powers through martial conquest of vengeful spirits
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deeply rooted in Japanese mythology and horror aesthetics; uses Japanese cultural imagery (tanuki characters, manji symbols, kanji numerals) as core identity; narrative framing as 'Japanesque Horror Action' positions horror as integrated with action mechanics rather than supplementary flavor

difficulty.lives_system: Player starts with three lives (Inochi); game ends when all lives depleted after health (Qi/Ki) reaches zero
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Limited; fixed three-life structure with no documented difficulty selection or adjustment mechanisms

progression.upgrade_system: Spell scroll acquisition system; defeating each boss grants new spell contained in scroll, expanding player moveset and tactical options
progression.unlock_mechanism: Boss-gated progression; each boss defeat unlocks one spell scroll, creating mandatory combat checkpoints for ability expansion
replayability_hooks: Multiple spell combinations to experiment with
Regional version differences (Japanese vs North American) create alternate playthroughs

world_building_techniques: Environmental setting: Tengumura Village and Cavern establish feudal Japanese location
Character-driven plot: Named protagonist Imoto with rescue mission for Shizuka
Mythological grounding: Vengeful spirits (Onryō) as boss enemies tied to Japanese folklore
Localization variance: English version altered kanji-based UI elements, suggesting cultural intentionality in narrative presentation

boss_design.philosophy: Bosses represent vengeful spirits (Onryō) that must be 'submitted' (Chōfuku); each defeat grants magical knowledge through spell scroll acquisition; defeat triggers on-screen text confirmation ('monster defeated')

faction_indicators: Tengumura Village community (NPC Mayor Akagi provides rescue mission briefing)
Implied conflict between human realm and spirit realm (abyss descent narrative)
danger_signals: Health depletion mechanic signals immediate character vulnerability
Abyss descent framing suggests escalating supernatural threat
environmental_storytelling: Tengumura Cavern (Kihōshōnyūdō in Japanese) serves as liminal space between village and supernatural abyss
Japanese feudal setting with mythological horror overlay creates cultural-specific atmosphere
Tanuki character presence (with cultural significance in Japanese folklore, altered in NA version) reinforces yokai/spirit world themes
